title: Labels: Teaching a Model the Right Answers
concept: A label is the correct answer attached to a training example, and a labeled dataset is what makes supervised learning possible.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the difference between regression and classification?

options.text: Regression predicts a number; classification predicts a category
They are exactly the same thing
Regression is for pictures; classification is for text

exercises.explain: Right! One outputs a number, the other outputs a category — both are supervised learning tasks.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Both regression and classification models need training data with correct answers attached.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: True! Both task types are forms of supervised learning, which always trains on labeled examples.

cards.title: Flashcards with answers on the back
cards.body: When you study with flashcards, each card has a question on the front and the correct answer on the back. A model learns the same way — by studying examples that come with the correct answer already attached.

cards.title: What is a label?
cards.body: A label is the correct answer attached to a piece of training data — like the word cat next to a photo, or the actual sale price next to a house's details. Data with labels is called labeled data.

cards.title: Price tags in a store
cards.body: Think of a label like a price tag on a store item. The tag doesn't change what the item is — it just tells you (or a model) the correct value to associate with it.

cards.title: Building a labeled dataset
cards.body: To teach a model to predict house prices, someone gathers thousands of past home sales — each one paired with its actual sold price, the label. To teach a model to spot spam, people collect emails and label each one spam or not spam.

cards.title: Labels don't attach themselves
cards.body: It's easy to assume labels magically come with the data. In reality, someone — often a real person — has to add them: tagging photos, marking emails, or recording final sale prices. This work is called data labeling, and it takes real time and effort.

left.text:
A photo of an animal
No answer attached

exercises.prompt: In a labeled dataset of emails, what would the label be?

options.text: Whether each email is spam or not spam
The email's file size
The font used in the email

exercises.explain: Right! The label is the correct answer the model is trying to learn to predict — spam or not spam.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Labels are always added automatically by a computer, with no human involved.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: False! Labels are very often added by real people — tagging photos, marking emails, and more.

exercises.sentence: Data that has the correct answer attached to each example is called ___ data.
exercises.explain: Labeled data has the correct answer attached to each example, which is what supervised learning needs.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is functioning as a label in a photo-tagging dataset?

options.text: The tag "cat" written under each photo
The photo's file format (jpg)
The camera's shutter speed setting

exercises.explain: The tag is the correct answer attached to the photo — that's the label. File format and shutter speed are just extra details, not the answer being predicted.

exercises.prompt: Why does a model need labeled examples to learn from in supervised learning?

options.text: So it can compare its guesses to the correct answers and improve
Labels make the data file smaller
Labels are only needed for regression, not classification
Labels are optional and rarely used

exercises.explain: Exactly! Without the correct answer to compare against, a model has no way to know if its guess was right.

exercises.prompt: A team is building a model to predict which job applicants are likely to get hired. To create their training data, they gather 10,000 past applications and note whether each applicant was actually hired or not. What role does the "hired / not hired" information play?

options.text: It's the label — the correct answer each example is paired with
It's a feature that describes the applicant's resume
It's irrelevant information that should be deleted
It's the model's prediction, not part of the training data

exercises.explain: Right! "Hired or not hired" is the correct answer for each past application — exactly what a label is.

exercises.prompt: Two teams build photo datasets. Team A's photos have no tags at all. Team B's photos are each tagged with the animal shown (dog, cat, bird). Which team's dataset could be used to train a supervised classification model, and why?

options.text: Team B, because supervised learning needs labeled examples with the correct answer attached
Team A, because unlabeled data trains models faster
Both teams, because labels don't matter for classification
Neither team, because photos can never be labeled

exercises.explain: Exactly! Without labels, there's no correct answer to learn from — Team B's tagged photos are what supervised learning requires.
